title: Stack ADT

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Stack ADT; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Stack ADT
sources.claim: A stack is a last-in-first-out abstract data type with push, pop, top, and emptiness contracts.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; material applicable to Stack ADT
sources.claim: A stack is a last-in-first-out abstract data type with push, pop, top, and emptiness contracts.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A stack is a last-in-first-out abstract data type with push, pop, top, and emptiness contracts.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Nested delimiters push opening symbols and require each closing symbol to match the current top. Your goal is to implement and test a stack and explain each operation's complexity. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Linked lists; Singly linked representation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A stack is a last-in-first-out abstract data type with push, pop, top, and emptiness contracts. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Pop on empty needs defined failure behavior, and array versus linked implementation changes capacity and allocation details. The draft therefore rejects: A stack permits removing any chosen middle element while preserving the ADT.

includes: Specify and implement push, pop, peek and empty behavior using an appropriate backing store.
Implement and test a stack and explain each operation's complexity.

In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
excludes: Pop on empty needs defined failure behavior, and array versus linked implementation changes capacity and allocation details.

outcomes: Implement and test a stack and explain each operation's complexity.

examples.title: Stack ADT: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Nested delimiters push opening symbols and require each closing symbol to match the current top.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Nested delimiters push opening symbols and require each closing symbol to match the current top.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Stack removal occurs only at the top under last-in-first-out order.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Stack removal occurs only at the top under last-in-first-out order.

examples.title: Stack ADT: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Pop on empty needs defined failure behavior, and array versus linked implementation changes capacity and allocation details.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A stack permits removing any chosen middle element while preserving the ADT.
misconceptions.correction: Stack removal occurs only at the top under last-in-first-out order.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: A stack permits removing any chosen middle element while preserving the ADT.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Stack ADT, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
Pop on empty needs defined failure behavior, and array versus linked implementation changes capacity and allocation details.

faded.prompt: Quick check for “Stack ADT: language-neutral trace”: state its starting evidence, apply the Stack ADT rule, and give the observable result in three short lines.
scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.
expectedEvidence: Named example: Stack ADT: language-neutral trace.
Starting evidence: Nested delimiters push opening symbols and require each closing symbol to match the current top.

Observable result: The conceptual result follows because Stack removal occurs only at the top under last-in-first-out order.
Boundary check: Stack removal occurs only at the top under last-in-first-out order.
